hypothesis: Keltner bands, which use ATR to define band width, adapt better to crypto volatility than static Bollinger bands; combining them with an ADX trend filter allows the strategy to take breakouts only in strong trends, while the ATR-based stop and take‑profit provide consistent risk scaling, reducing the high drawdown seen with the previous Bollinger‑based logic.

In volatile, trend‑driven periods (high ADX), breaking a Keltner band captures momentum, while in low‑trend periods (low ADX) price tends to revert to the band center; ATR scaling ensures risk is proportional to market volatility.

Combining Bollinger band breakouts with ADX trend strength allows the strategy to trade true breakouts in volatile regimes while reverting to mean in weak trends; ATR-based risk management keeps exposure proportional to market volatility, improving risk‑adjusted returns.

Combining a Keltner breakout with an ADX trend strength filter and an RSI momentum filter limits entries to strong directional moves, reducing whipsaws and overtrading while ATR-based risk management keeps position sizing appropriate to market volatility.

Using ADX to distinguish trending from ranging markets allows the strategy to apply a breakout rule in strong trends and a mean‑reversion rule when the market is weak; Keltner bands provide dynamic price levels that adapt to volatility while ATR offers a consistent risk scale, ensuring that stops and targets are proportional to recent price movement.
